title: AI and the Future of Cyber Careers

modules.title: Module 1: What AI Is Actually Automating in Security Operations Today
modules.sub: Separating hype from reality in AI-driven SOC automation
modules.description: Learners examine which specific SOC tasks — alert triage, log analysis, threat correlation — are already being handled by AI tools in practice. They assess real deployment examples to distinguish genuine automation from vendor overstatement, building a grounded baseline for evaluating their own role's exposure.

modules.title: Module 2: The New Threat Surface — Jobs Created by AI-Driven Attacks
modules.sub: Understanding how AI-powered attacks are generating new security demand
modules.description: Learners analyze how adversaries are weaponizing AI to accelerate phishing, malware generation, and reconnaissance, and map the defensive gaps these tactics create. By the end, they can articulate why AI-driven threats are expanding — not shrinking — the need for skilled human security professionals in specific areas.

modules.title: Module 3: Skills That Remain Irreplaceable — Judgment, Adversarial Thinking, and Context
modules.sub: Identifying the human capabilities AI tools cannot replicate
modules.description: Learners evaluate why contextual judgment, creative adversarial reasoning, and organizational knowledge resist automation, using case studies where AI tools failed without human oversight. They reflect on which of these durable skills they currently possess and which require deliberate development.

modules.title: Module 4: Emerging Roles — AI Red Teamer, ML Security Engineer, Prompt Auditor
modules.sub: Mapping the new job categories forming at the AI-security intersection
modules.description: Learners explore the responsibilities, required skills, and career pathways for roles that didn't exist five years ago, including AI red teamers, ML security engineers, and prompt auditors. They compare these profiles against their current background to identify which emerging role represents the most viable pivot or growth direction for them personally.

modules.title: Module 5: Reskilling Roadmap — Where to Invest Time and Credentials Now
modules.sub: Prioritizing learning investments that pay off in an AI-augmented field
modules.description: Learners evaluate competing credentials, training programs, and self-study paths against the actual skill gaps identified in earlier modules, applying a prioritization framework based on role relevance, time cost, and market signal. They produce a draft personal reskilling roadmap with a sequenced 12-month action plan.

modules.title: Module 6: How Organizations Are Restructuring Security Teams Around AI Tools
modules.sub: Understanding team redesign decisions that affect roles and reporting structures
modules.description: Learners examine how security leadership is reorganizing headcount, workflows, and responsibilities as AI tools absorb routine tasks, including patterns of role consolidation and new specialist insertion. They analyze what these structural shifts mean for career advancement, team positioning, and the skills that earn influence in a restructured security organization.

modules.title: Module 7: Building a Career Strategy That Holds Up as the Field Keeps Shifting
modules.sub: Designing an adaptive career plan built for ongoing uncertainty
modules.description: Learners synthesize insights from the full course into a coherent, personalized career strategy that accounts for continued AI-driven change rather than assuming a stable endpoint. They stress-test their plan against plausible future scenarios — including further automation, new threat categories, and organizational shifts — and identify the early signals they will monitor to know when to adjust.

synopsis: AI is reshaping which cybersecurity tasks get automated, which roles get eliminated, and which new specializations are urgently needed — but most career guidance hasn't caught up with what's actually happening inside security operations centers. This course gives both working professionals and curious non-specialists an honest, evidence-based picture of where the field is heading and what to do about it.
rationale: Widespread anxiety about AI replacing security jobs is driving poor career decisions, while the real story — significant role transformation rather than wholesale elimination — remains underexplained.
learning_outcome: Learners can identify which parts of their current or target security role are most exposed to automation and build a concrete, prioritized plan for skill development that reflects the AI-augmented security landscape.
source_signals: Will AI Replace Cyber Security Jobs? | Is AI Going To Replace Cybersecurity ?
Comparing AI agents to cybersecurity professionals in real-world pen testing
